title: EEG Array and Headframe Implant Surgery
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"9nl3c","text":"This protocol describes the surgical procedures necessary to implant a surface EEG Array and Headframe in an adult mouse. This surgery is necessary to perform simultaneous recordings of EEG and Neuropixels from a head-fixed mouse in-vivo.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"2rtcm","text":"Only perform this procedure in accordance with IACUC and veterinary requirements.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
before_start: {"blocks":[{"key":"5pruk","text":"Prior to beginning, obtain a surface multi-electrode array. We order a custom version of the EEG-mouse-30-A-8 surface array without the posterior square reference electrode from NeuroNexus. Alternatively, you could remove the reference electrode with microscissors while preparing the EEG array. During the surgery, we use a custom headframe and various custom tools; references for these items can be found in the Materials section.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
warning: {"blocks":[{"key":"49eqq","text":"Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) should be used at all times while operating this protocol.\n","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"46jd9","text":"Isoflurane Warning: Acute over-exposure to waste anesthetic gases (WAG) may cause eye irritation, headache, nausea, drowsiness or dizziness. Repeated exposure may cause damage to cardiovascular system and central nervous system. Refer to MSDS for additional information.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"3o359","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"d97p5","text":"Use caution when operating soldering iron to avoid burns. steps: Separate and straighten the 3 wires attached to the printed circuit board (PCB) connector and cut to desired length (~4 cm). The ground wire is green and the reference wires are white (channel R1) and blue (channel R2).
Sterilize the prepped EEG array and the soldered screws by soaking them in 70% ethanol for 5 minutes, being careful not to soak the PCB and pin out connector.
Administer up to 1.0 mL of Lactated Ringer's Solution via subcutaneous injection.

Strip the wire insulation about 0.5 cm from the end of each wire. Wrap the exposed wire around the screw where the head meets the body. Photo of the EEG array. The end of the blue wire is stripped of insulation and the exposed wire is wrapped around the screw in preparation for soldering.

Using microscissors, make small, straight incisions into the polyimide where the bottom 3 fingers of electrodes connect to the array on both sides (avoid cutting the embedded wires). This allows the array to lay flatter on the curved surface of the skull when implanted on the mouse. Photo of the EEG array. Red dashed lines on the right side of the array indicate where incisions should be made in the polyimide. Faint lines can be seen on the left side of the array where the incisions have been made.
Add drops of Artificial Cerebrospinal Fluid (ACSF) to the surface of the skull and place the EEG array on top of the ACSF, with electrodes facing down.

Add a thin layer of Kwik-Cast on top of the array and to the bottom of the ribbon cable. Allow Kwik-Cast to cure completely. The Kwik-Cast should cover the array and extend onto the skull surface with a ~0.2 mm border around the array to keep it adhered to the skull.
Lay a piece of gauze on top of the Kwik-Cast and carefully flip the PCB onto the gauze.

Use an FG 1/4 drill bit to drill 3 burr holes in the skull at the marked locations on the cerebellum. Ensure that the diameter of the drill bit is similar to the diameter of the screws you will use. If you make the hole too large, the threads will not bite in the skull and the screw will be loose or fall out. If you make the hole too small, you will not be able to screw the screw into the skull.

Add the dovetail clamp (with the headframe attached to it) back onto the stereotaxic frame. Lower the headframe to -21 D/V, 0 A/P, 0 M/L. Illustration showing the headframe in place and a thin layer of Kwik-Cast on top of the EEG array. Cement is covering the skull screws, but cement has not yet been applied over the top of the Kwik-Cast and to secure the headframe. Note: dovetail clamp holding the headframe in place is not pictured in this step.

Prepare surgical space, anesthetize the mouse, and prepare the mouse for surgery as per steps 1-6 and 25-35 of the "General Setup and Takedown Procedures for Rodent Neurosurgery" protocol linked below. General Setup and Takedown Procedures for Rodent Neurosurgery by Avalon Amaya
Allow the mouse to recover in the home cage placed on a 38 °C heating pad.
Administer post-operative injections as specified in IACUC protocol. Allow the mouse to recover for 1 week before performing experiments.
Expose and prepare the skull surface as per steps 1-4 of the "Multi-Site Optic Fiber Implants" protocol linked below. Multi-Site Optic Fiber Implants by Avalon Amaya

Lower the headframe to -21 D/V, without moving the A/P or M/L manipulators. The left side of the headframe should just barely touch the skin. The headframe should not be putting pressure on the skin or the surface of skull.

Bring the headframe back to 0 D/V and keep the A/P and M/L manipulators at 0. Remove the dovetail clamp and place it aside until later. To keep the stereotaxic manipulators zeroed at bregma, do not move the manipulators until step 32.
After the cement has hardened, unclamp the headframe from the dovetail and bring the dovetail back to 0 D/V, 0 A/P 0, M/L.
Lower the bregma stylus to the surface of the cement and use a fine point sharpie to draw a dot on the surface of the cement at the location of bregma.
Obtain the postoperative weight of the mouse.
Cut the white reference jumper wire loop off. This allows the white and blue reference wires to be used as independent external references.
Add epoxy to the exposed jumper wire ends, and to the base of each of the 3 insulated wires.

Add a drop of flux to the wire and solder the wire to the screw. Ensure at least 2-3 threads are still visible and not covered by wire or solder. Repeat for all 3 wires. After soldering, clean any remaining flux from the screws by rinsing in 70% ethanol and then water. Do not exceed 660 °F for the soldering temperature as this could damage the PCB. Photo of the blue wire soldered to the screw. Solder only covers the top 2-3 threads so that there are still available threads that can be screwed into the skull.
Soak the EEG array and screws in sterile water for 5 minutes and air dry before implanting.

Use a fine point sharpie to draw a dot on bregma. Move the micromanipulator to each of the coordinates where craniotomies will be drilled for insertion of Neuropixels probes. Mark a dot on each of these locations.
Position the dovetail clamp and bregma stylus over bregma again (lightly touching the surface of the skull). Zero all stereotaxic manipulators on the digital display.

Keep the A/P and M/L manipulators at 0, and drive the bregma stylus in the dorsal direction away from the surface of the skull. Remove the bregma stylus and attach the titanium EEG headframe to the dovetail clamp.

Using a fine point sharpie, add 3 dots to the skull over the cerebellum (located posterior to the lambda suture). These dots mark the locations to implant the skull screws and should be spaced about 1-2mm away from each other. Ensure there is enough room between the dots so that the screws will not touch when implanted.

Use the 4 pre-made holes in the array to adjust the position. Once the + symbol on the EEG array is centered on top of bregma, use a cotton or sugi swab to absorb the excess ACSF. The array should adhere to the surface of the skull. If the EEG array needs to be moved after the ACSF has been dried, add more ACSF to release the array from the skull before attempting to move it. Illustration showing EEG array on the surface of the skull.

Being careful not to damage the underlying brain tissue, screw each of the skull screws into the pre-drilled holes. Screws should be firmly set in the skull, in contact with the CSF, but not protruding into the brain tissue. Screws should not be touching one another or else they will short the ground and reference connections. Illustration showing PCB flipped onto a piece of gauze on top of the EEG array. The screw attached to the green wire is screwed into the skull above the right cerebellum.

Use dental cement to adhere the headframe to the skull. Flip the PCB onto the headfame. Then cover all exposed skull, and the Kwik-Cast encapsulated array, with dental cement. Extend the dental cement ~1 mm over the cut skin all around to enclose the incision.
Add the bregma stylus to the dovetail clamp, which should still be zeroed over bregma.
Move the micromanipulator to each of the coordinates where craniotomies will be drilled for insertion of Neuropixels probes, and mark a dot on each of these locations.

If any part of the ribbon cable is still exposed, cover it with Kwik-Cast. Tidy up the exposed wires from the skull screws: tuck the wires as close as possible to the cement/PCB and cover with Kwik-Cast. Illustration showing the final product of the surgery: a mouse with a headframe and EEG implanted on the skull.
Turn off the isoflurane.
Gently remove the mouse from ear bars and bite bar.
Follow steps 36-46 as per the "General Setup and Takedown Procedures for Rodent Neurosurgery" protocol.

Using a scalpel blade, carefully score the skull around the edges to ensure cement will adhere to skull. Do not score where the electrodes will sit on the skull. Do not score close enough to the edge that it interferes with the Vetbond seal.

Follow steps 5-6 of the "Multi-Site Optic Fiber Implants" protocol to level and align the skull. When locating bregma, it can be useful to apply a few drops of 3% hydrogen peroxide to the surface of the skull and use a cotton swab to lightly rub the area near bregma. The sutures will appear white after applying hydrogen peroxide, making them easier to identify.
Cover the skull screws and the rest of the skull over the cerebellum with dental cement.

Epoxy a small nut in the center of the PCB on the side of the array where the wires are connected. This nut goes in the hollow square of the EEG headframe and stabilizes the connection between the PCB and the headframe. Photo of the EEG array fully prepped and ready for implantation. The nut is epoxied to the PCB.

After the mouse has recovered from surgery (minimum 1 week), test the EEG signal to ensure that it looks good before proceeding with the experiment. Check the quality of the signals: examine the raw signals for excessive high frequency, motion-related noise plot the frequency spectrum to look for contamination by 50/60 Hz line noise evaluate the signal-to-noise ratio of an event-related potential to an event of interest (e.g., visual or auditory stimulus) Common issues that lead to noise or low amplitude signals are: loose ground/reference screw(s) ground/reference screw(s) that does not penetrate the skull bad solder joint with ground/reference screw(s) Kwik-Cast or fluid between the skull and electrode(s) ground loops in the recording system

After screws are screwed into the skull, check for stability. The screws should be firmly set in the skull and should not move when gently nudged with forceps. A loose screw can be a source of noise in the EEG and/or Neuropixels signals.

Set up a way to externally access the 3 skull screws you have implanted in the mouse. One method for doing this is to join 2 Omnetics connectors together, keeping the wires for the R1, R2, and Ground pins free. Having access to these connections allows you to choose which skull screw to use for each connection (R1, R2, and Ground).

As an optional quality control step, use an impedance testing device to measure the impedance between two of the screws at a time. The impedance between any 2 screws should be below 5kΩ to be considered a good ground/reference. High impedance of the ground and reference electrodes can lead to poor signal to noise ratio in the recorded signals.
Align the PCB with the headframe and ensure that the nut is sitting in the hollow square of the headframe. Secure PCB to the headframe using a zip-tie.
creator: Lydia C. Marks
